failure_modes.text: Temporary coexistence can quietly become the mechanism that keeps the organization trapped in the old path.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when leaders treat "temporary coexistence" as harmless implementation detail.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set an explicit sunset date for temporary coexistence; without one, interim arrangements quietly become the permanent path.

failure_modes.text: Teams can price switching later as if it were the same option as switching now even while reversal cost is compounding.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when switching later is priced as if it were the same option as switching now.
failure_modes.mitigation: Price switching cost from today's position, not from a future hypothetical; reversal cost compounds while the team deliberates.

failure_modes.text: Lock-in can outlive fit when a path is defended mainly because departure feels costly or identity-threatening.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate the path's actual fit from the emotional or identity cost of departure; lock-in that outlives fit is defended by sunk-cost logic.

premortem_questions.text: What would you have to believe for the current sticky path to still be the best choice a year from now?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

premortem_questions.text: If switching later were materially harder than switching now, where would that difficulty already be coming from?
premortem_questions.source_quote: The option set narrows long before the plan admits it.

premortem_questions.text: What part of the current plan assumes reversibility that coexistence, retraining, or local workflow drift is already eroding?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Dual-run periods, exception handling, retraining, and partner workarounds can become the mechanism that locks the organization into the old path while everyone still speaks as if the switch remains easy.

heuristics.text: Decide deliberately which norms or systems should become sticky and which must stay revisable.
heuristics.source_quote: Lock-in becomes productive when leaders consciously choose which norms, systems, or strategic positions should remain sticky and which must stay revisable.

heuristics.text: Treat dual-run coexistence as a real cost center instead of as harmless implementation detail.
heuristics.source_quote: The real economic and operational burden comes from keeping both the old and new path alive at the same time.

heuristics.text: Test whether the option to switch later survives time instead of assuming the future option set will stay open.
heuristics.source_quote: Best used when teams say "we can switch later" without testing whether the option survives time.

curation_notes.summary: The source is strongest on coexistence drag, compounding switching cost, and deliberate stickiness rather than on generic consistency language.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor migration-policy overlays that widened beyond the raw source's clearer path-dependence and reversal-cost material.
open_questions: The first premortem question generalizes the source challenge language so it stays reusable beyond infrastructure migration cases.

allies: The raw allies section is strong on checklists and cognitive-load-theory as beneficial forms of deliberate stickiness for later relation preservation.
antagonists: The raw antagonists section is strongest on first-principles-thinking and multidimensional reframing, but only the cleanest current-vocabulary ids should survive Wave 3.
structured_tensions: The source contains an explicit structured tension block naming optionality and first-principles-thinking.
